subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Assertion A: A bar magnet dropped through a metallic cylindrical pipe takes more time to come down compared to a non-magnetic bar with same geometry and mass.
Reason R: For the magnetic bar, Eddy currents are produced in the metallic pipe which oppose the motion of the magnetic bar.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Assertion A: A bar magnet dropped through a metallic cylindrical pipe takes more time to come down compared to a non-magnetic bar with same geometry and mass.
Reason R: For the magnetic bar, Eddy currents are produced in the metallic pipe which oppose the motion of the magnetic bar.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is true

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and R are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is false

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
solution:
Due to change in flux, eddy current is produced which opposes the motion in the metallic pipe for the bar magnet.

Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday's law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

Hence, R is the correct explanation and both statements are true.

subject: Physics
q: A metallic rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in North-South direction and is moved at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards East. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at that place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced in the rod is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
question: A metallic rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in North-South direction and is moved at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards East. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at that place is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The emf induced in the rod is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
solution:
Emf produced is ε=BVL⊥v⊥

We know BVBH=tan45°, so BV=4×10-3 T

Then,  ε=4×10-3×20×10-2×20 =16 mV

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The diamagnetic property depends on temperature.

Statement II : The induced magnetic dipole moment in a diamagnetic sample is always opposite to the magnetising field.

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : The diamagnetic property depends on temperature.

Statement II : The induced magnetic dipole moment in a diamagnetic sample is always opposite to the magnetising field.

In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are False
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
The properties of a diamagnetic material are as follows:

1. The susceptibility has a low negative value.

2. Susceptibility is independent of temperature.

3. The relative permeability is slightly less than one.

4. When placed in a non-uniform magnetic field they have a tendency to move away from the field.

5. When suspended freely in a uniform magnetic field, they set themselves perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field.

Hence, Statement I is false, but statement II is true.

subject: Physics
q:
Two short bar magnets of length 1cm each have magnetic moments 1.20 A m2 and 1.00 A m2 respectively. They are placed on a horizontal table parallel to each other with their N poles pointing towards the south. They have a common magnetic equator and are separated by a distance of 20.0 cm. The value of the resultant horizontal magnetic induction at the mid-point O of the line joining their centres is close to
( Horizontal component of earth's magnetic induction is 3.6×10-5  Wb m-2 )
question:
Two short bar magnets of length 1cm each have magnetic moments 1.20 A m2 and 1.00 A m2 respectively. They are placed on a horizontal table parallel to each other with their N poles pointing towards the south. They have a common magnetic equator and are separated by a distance of 20.0 cm. The value of the resultant horizontal magnetic induction at the mid-point O of the line joining their centres is close to
( Horizontal component of earth's magnetic induction is 3.6×10-5  Wb m-2 )
solution:
B net = B 1 + B 2 + B H

= μ 0 4 π M 1 x 3 + μ 0 4 π M 2 x 3 + B H

= { 10 −7 ( 1.2+1 ) } ( 0.1 ) 3 +3.6× 10 −5

= 2.56 × 10 - 4  Wb / m 2

subject: Physics
q: The space inside a straight current carrying solenoid is filled with a magnetic material having magnetic susceptibility equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is fractional increase in the magnetic field inside solenoid with respect to air as medium inside the solenoid?
question: The space inside a straight current carrying solenoid is filled with a magnetic material having magnetic susceptibility equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is fractional increase in the magnetic field inside solenoid with respect to air as medium inside the solenoid?
solution:
Given here, χ=1.2×10-5

Magnetic field inside the material in solenoid is B'=μ01+χni, here, i is current.

Now, magnetic field with air as medium inside solenoid is B=μ0ni.

So, fractional increase is B'-BB=χ=1.2×10-5

subject: Physics
q: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
question: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
options: to oscillate the coil in magnetic field for longer period of time
to bring the coil to rest quickly
to produce large deflecting torque on the coil
to make the magnetic field radial
solution: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of nonmagnetic metallic material. When the coil oscillates, the eddy currents generated in the core oppose the motion and bring the coil to rest quickly. Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday’s law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

subject: Physics

question: A magnetic needle lying parallel to a magnetic field requires $\mathrm{W}$ units of work to turn it through $60^{\circ}$. The torque needed to maintain the needle in this position will be
solution: $\tau=(\mathrm{H}) \tan 60^{\circ} \quad=\mathrm{W} \cdot \sqrt{3}$

question: A solenoid having area $A$ and length ' $l$ ' is filled with a material having relative permeability 2. The magnetic energy stored in the solenoid is :
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{2 \mu_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{u}_0} \Rightarrow \mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{4 \mu_0} \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{4 \mu_0} \mathrm{~A} \ell$

subject: Physics
q: Two coils <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mi>'</mi></math> are separated by some distance. When a current of <math><mn>3</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows through coil <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> a magnetic flux of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> No current is passed through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When no current passes through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and a current of <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi>Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the flux through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> is:
question: Two coils <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mi>'</mi></math> are separated by some distance. When a current of <math><mn>3</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows through coil <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> a magnetic flux of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> No current is passed through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When no current passes through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and a current of <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi>Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the flux through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> is:
solution:
Since the coils are co-axial, the  flux through the coils can be written as the product of their surface area and the magnetic field produced by the coil along its axis.

Consider the flux passing through Q

ϕQ=μ0i1R22R2+x232×πR2=10-3 Wb

 

And the flux passing through P:
ϕP=μ0i1r22R2+x232×πR2

Taking the ratio
ϕPϕQ=i2i1×R2+x232R2+x232⇒ϕP10-3=i2i1
⇒ϕP10-3=23
∴ϕP=6.67×10-4 Wb

question: The mid points of two small magnetic dipoles of length d in end-on positions, are separated by a distance xx≫d. The magnitude of force between them is proportional to x−n where n is :

F=− dU dx = −3μo 4π 2 M 2 x 4

therefore force between them ∝ x - 4

subject: Physics
q:
A short bar magnet is placed in the magnetic meridian of the earth with North Pole pointing north. Neutral points are found at a distance of 30 cm from the magnet on the East-West line, drawn through the middle point of the magnet. The magnetic moment of the magnet in Am2 is close to:

(Given μ04π=10-7 in SI units and BH= Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field =3.6×10-5 Tesla.)
question:
A short bar magnet is placed in the magnetic meridian of the earth with North Pole pointing north. Neutral points are found at a distance of 30 cm from the magnet on the East-West line, drawn through the middle point of the magnet. The magnetic moment of the magnet in Am2 is close to:

(Given μ04π=10-7 in SI units and BH= Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field =3.6×10-5 Tesla.)
solution:
Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is along geographical south its North.

M→= Magnetic dipole moment.



At neutral point on the equatorial line the magnetic field B′ cancels BH

∴B′=BH

⇒μ04πMx3=BH 

⇒10-7×M301003=3.6×10-5

⇒10-7×103×M27=3.6×10-5

M=27×3.6×10-510-4

M=27×3.6×10-1

=9.72

M≈9.7

subject: Physics
q: The length of a magnet is large compared to its width and breadth. The time period of its width and breadth. The time period of its oscillation in a vibration magnetometer is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The magnet is cut along its length into three equal parts and three parts are then placed on each other with their like poles together. The time period of this combination will be
question: The length of a magnet is large compared to its width and breadth. The time period of its width and breadth. The time period of its oscillation in a vibration magnetometer is $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The magnet is cut along its length into three equal parts and three parts are then placed on each other with their like poles together. The time period of this combination will be
solution:
Time period of vibration, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{M B}}$
Where $\ell=$ moment of inertia of magnet, $\mathrm{M}=$ magnetic moment $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \ell^2}{12}$ and $\mathrm{M}=$ pole strength $\times \ell$
$$
I^{\prime}=\frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{m}{3}\right)\left(\frac{\ell}{3}\right)^2 \times 3=\frac{\mathrm{l}}{9}
$$
and $M^{\prime}=$ pole strength (will remain the same) $\times(\ell / 3) \times 3=M$.
$$
\mathrm{T}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\sqrt{9}}=\frac{2}{9} \mathrm{~s} .
$$

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet of length $6 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a magnetic moment of $4 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}$. Find the strength of magnetic field at a distance of $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the magnet along its equatorial line.
question: A bar magnet of length $6 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a magnetic moment of $4 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}$. Find the strength of magnetic field at a distance of $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the magnet along its equatorial line.
solution:
Along the equatorial line, magnetic field strength
$$
\left.B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{M}{\left(r^2+\ell^2{ }^{3 / 2}\right.}\right)
$$
Given: $M=4 \mathrm{~J} T^{-1}$
$$
r=200 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \mathrm{~m}
$$
$\ell=\frac{6 \mathrm{~cm}}{2}=3 \mathrm{~cm}=3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$$
\therefore B=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{4}{\left[2^2+\left(3 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2\right]^{3 / 2}}
$$
Solving we get, $B=5 \times 10^{-8}$ tesla

q: An aeroplane, with its wings spread <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is flying at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal direction. question: An aeroplane, with its wings spread <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is flying at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal direction. The total intensity of earth's field at that part is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The emf induced between the tips of the plane wings will be
solution:
∈=B→V→L→=BVLsinθ

=2.5×10-4 T180×518 m s-110 msin60°

=108.25×10-3 V

subject: Physics
q:
Statement I : The ferromagnetic property depends on temperature. At high temperature, ferromagnet becomes paramagnet.

Statement II : At high temperature, the domain wall area of a ferromagnetic substance increases. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Statement I : The ferromagnetic property depends on temperature. At high temperature, ferromagnet becomes paramagnet.

Statement II : At high temperature, the domain wall area of a ferromagnetic substance increases. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
solution: As temperature increases, domains disintegrate so ferromagnetism decreases and above curie temperature it become paramagnet.

subject: Physics
q: A perfectly diamagnetic sphere has a small spherical cavity at its centre, which is filled with a paramagnetic substance. The whole system is placed in a uniform magnetic field B→. Then the field inside the paramagnetic substance is:
question: A perfectly diamagnetic sphere has a small spherical cavity at its centre, which is filled with a paramagnetic substance. The whole system is placed in a uniform magnetic field B→. Then the field inside the paramagnetic substance is:
options: much large than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced></math> and parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>

much large than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced></math> but opposite to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>
solution: When magnetic field is applied diamagnetic substance produces magnetic field in opposite direction so net magnetic field will be zero.

subject: Physics
q: The B-H curve for a ferromagnet is shown in the figure. The ferromagnet is placed inside a long solenoid with 1000 turns/cm.. The current that should be passed in the solenoid to demagnetize the ferromagnet completely is:
question: The B-H curve for a ferromagnet is shown in the figure. The ferromagnet is placed inside a long solenoid with 1000 turns/cm.. The current that should be passed in the solenoid to demagnetize the ferromagnet completely is:
solution:
Given Number of turns, $\mathrm{n}=1000$ turns $/ \mathrm{cm}=1000 \times 100$ turns $/ \mathrm{m}$ Coercivity of ferromagnet, $\mathrm{H}=100 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ Current to demagnetise the ferromagnet, $\mathrm{I}=?$
Using, $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{nI}$
or, $\quad 100=10^5 \times \mathrm{I}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{100}{10^5}=1 \mathrm{~mA}$

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field of earth at the equator is approximately <math> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mtext>T</mtext> </mrow> </math>. The radius of earth is <math> <mrow> <mtext>6.4</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mn>6</mn> </msup> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>. Then the dipole moment of the earth will be nearly of the order of :
question: The magnetic field of earth at the equator is approximately <math> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mtext>T</mtext> </mrow> </math>. The radius of earth is <math> <mrow> <mtext>6.4</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mn>6</mn> </msup> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>. Then the dipole moment of the earth will be nearly of the order of :
solution:
B = μ 0 4 π · M d 3 = 1 0 - 7 × M 6.4 × 1 0 6 3

∴    4 × 1 0 - 5 = 1 0 - 7 × M 6.4 × 1 0 6 3

∴    M = 6.4 × 1 0 6 3 × 4 × 1 0 - 5 1 0 - 7

          = 6.4 3 × 1 0 1 8 × 4 × 1 0 2

          = 6.4 3 × 4 × 1 0 2 0

          = 1 . 0 4 8 × 1 0 2 3 ≈ 10 23  A m 2

subject: Physics
q: In a ferromagnetic material, below the curie temperature, a domain is defined as:
question: In a ferromagnetic material, below the curie temperature, a domain is defined as:
options: a macroscopic region with saturation magnetization.
a macroscopic region with consecutive magnetic dipoles oriented in opposite direction.
a macroscopic region with zero magnetization.
a macroscopic region with randomly oriented magnetic dipoles.
solution: In a ferromagnetic material, below the curie temperature, a domain is defined as a macroscopic region with saturation magnetization.

subject: Physics
q: A magnetic compass needle oscillates <math><mn>30</mn></math> times per minute at a place where the dip is <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math><mn>40</mn></math> times per minute where the dip is <math><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the net magnetic fields due to the earth at the two places respectively, then the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately equal to
question: A magnetic compass needle oscillates <math><mn>30</mn></math> times per minute at a place where the dip is <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math><mn>40</mn></math> times per minute where the dip is <math><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the net magnetic fields due to the earth at the two places respectively, then the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately equal to
solution:
Let f1 and f2 be the respective oscillation frequencies respectively for 45° and 30° dip. Then,

f1=12πMB1cos45°I

f2=12πMB2cos30°I

f1f2=B1cos45°B2cos30°

⇒302402=B1cos45°B2cos30° 

⇒B1B2=900×cos30°1600×cos45°

⇒B1B2=0.688≈0.7

subject: Physics
q: The free space inside a current carrying toroid is filled with a material of susceptibility 2×10-2. The percentage increase in the value of magnetic field inside the toroid will be
question: The free space inside a current carrying toroid is filled with a material of susceptibility 2×10-2. The percentage increase in the value of magnetic field inside the toroid will be
solution:
It is given that 

χm=2×10-2

The permeability is μr=1+χm=1+0.02=1.02.

The magnetic field is B=μrB0=1.02B0

Hence, percentage increase in magnetic field is 

B-B0B0×100%=B01.02-1B0×100%=2%

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in the magnetic meridian with its north pole pointing towards the geographic north pole. A neutral point is obtained at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the center of the magnet. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>H</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></math>, the magnetic moment of the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A bar magnet of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in the magnetic meridian with its north pole pointing towards the geographic north pole. A neutral point is obtained at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the center of the magnet. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mi>H</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi></math>, the magnetic moment of the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
i.e. 2μ04πmr2×7r=0.4×10-4

⇒2×10-7×m×772+1823/2×104=0.4×10-4

m=4×10-2×3733/214

⇒M=m×14 cm=m×14100=0.04×3733/214×14100

⇒M=4×10-4×7203.82=2.88 J T-1.

subject: Physics
q:
The relationship between the magnetic susceptibility $(\chi)$ and the magnetic permeability $(\mu)$ is given by :
($\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space and $\mu_{\mathrm{r}}$ is relative permeability)
question:
The relationship between the magnetic susceptibility $(\chi)$ and the magnetic permeability $(\mu)$ is given by :
($\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space and $\mu_{\mathrm{r}}$ is relative permeability)
solution:
We have
$\begin{aligned} & \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=(1+\chi) \Rightarrow \chi=\left(\mu_{\mathrm{r}}-1\right) \\ & \mu=\mu_0 \mu_{\mathrm{r}} \Rightarrow \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} \\ & \therefore \chi=\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_0}-1\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason(R).
Assertion (A) : Magnetic monopoles do not exist.
Reason (R) : Magnetic field lines are continuous and form closed loops.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason(R).
Assertion (A) : Magnetic monopoles do not exist.
Reason (R) : Magnetic field lines are continuous and form closed loops.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct
Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct and $(\mathrm{R})$ is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is not correct but ( $\mathrm{R}$) is correct
solution: Both statements are correct and reason is also the correct explanation of assertion.

subject: Physics
q: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math>. The permeability of the material in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, is [Permeability of free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>]
question: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math>. The permeability of the material in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, is [Permeability of free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>]
solution:
Using the relation of magnetic susceptibility χ and relative permeabilityμr.

μr=1+χ=1+99=100

Magnetic permeability, μ=μ0μr

=4π×10-7×100

μ=4π×10-5

subject: Physics
q: The current required to be passed through a solenoid of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> turns in order to demagnetise a bar magnet of magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: The current required to be passed through a solenoid of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> turns in order to demagnetise a bar magnet of magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
The magnetic intensity is

H=Bμ0-M

For M=0, it can be written

H=Bμ0=ni

The data given is 

H=2.4×103 A m-1l=15×10-2 mN=60

Using the relation, n=Nl and H=ni, the value of the current is,

i=Hn=2.4×103×15×10-260=6 A

subject: Physics
q: At some location  the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> At this location, magnetic needle of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.12</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and pole strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Am</mi></math> is suspended from its mid-point using a thread, it makes <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angles with horizontal in equilibrium. To keep this needle horizontal, the vertical force that should be applied at one of its ends is:
question: At some location  the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> At this location, magnetic needle of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.12</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and pole strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Am</mi></math> is suspended from its mid-point using a thread, it makes <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angles with horizontal in equilibrium. To keep this needle horizontal, the vertical force that should be applied at one of its ends is:
solution:
The horizontal and vertical components of earth's magnetic field (BH and BV) are related as

BVBH=tanθ

Here, θ=45° and BH=18×10-6 T

⇒BV=BHtan45°

⇒BV=BH=18×10-6 T   ∵ tan45°=1

Now, when the external force F is applied, to keep the needle stays in horizontal position is shown below,

Taking torque at point P, we get

mBV×2l=Fl

∴ F=2×mBV

Substituting the given values, we get

F=2×1.8×18×10-6

F=6.48×10-5=6.5×10-5 N.
